questions.q: What does AABB (Axis-Aligned Bounding Box) collision detection check?
options: Whether two rotated boxes overlap at any angle
Whether two axis-aligned rectangles/boxes overlap
Whether a point is inside a circle
Whether a moving object crossed a plane
questions.explanation: AABB checks if two axis-aligned (non-rotated) boxes occupy the same space by comparing their min/max coordinates on each axis. It's fast and commonly used for broad-phase detection, though it can't handle rotated shapes.

questions.q: In a rigid body physics engine, what does numerical integration do?
options: Converts collision shapes from one format to another
Updates object position and velocity over time using forces and acceleration
Stores collision history for replay systems
Calculates the total mass of compound bodies
questions.explanation: Numerical integration (e.g., Euler or Verlet) computes new position from velocity and acceleration, and new velocity from applied forces, stepping the simulation forward in time. This is the core of physics updates.

questions.q: What is tunneling in physics simulation and how does it occur?
options: Objects getting stuck inside terrain due to high friction
A fast-moving object passing through a solid surface due to insufficient collision checks between frames
Gravity pulling objects in unintended directions
Physics updates taking longer than expected each frame
questions.explanation: Tunneling happens when an object moves far enough between physics frames to pass through thin colliders (e.g., a bullet crosses a wall without hitting it). Smaller timesteps or continuous collision detection prevent this.

questions.q: What is the difference between continuous and discrete collision detection?
options: Continuous is slower but checks all object pairs; discrete is faster but misses fast-moving collisions
Discrete checks collisions at each frame; continuous predicts collisions along the path of motion
Continuous only works with spheres; discrete works with all shapes
Discrete is used for network games; continuous is used for local games
questions.explanation: Discrete collision detection checks static positions at each timestep and can miss fast-moving collisions. Continuous collision detection predicts along the motion path or uses swept shapes, catching collisions that would tunnel.

questions.q: In a 2D raycasting system, what does a raycast return?
options: All objects within a sphere around the ray origin
The first object the ray intersects and at what distance
The surface normal where the ray starts
The velocity of objects near the ray
questions.explanation: A raycast fires an infinite line from a point in a direction and returns the nearest intersected object and hit distance. Raycasts are used for line-of-sight checks, shooting, and sensor systems.

questions.q: What is a kinematic rigidbody and how does it differ from a dynamic one?
options: Kinematic is heavier and falls faster than dynamic
Kinematic bodies are moved by scripts/animation without physics forces; dynamic bodies respond to gravity and collisions
Kinematic bodies can only detect collisions; dynamic bodies collide
Kinematic is used for bullets; dynamic is used for players
questions.explanation: Kinematic bodies have no physics simulation—they move directly via script or animation and trigger collision events. Dynamic bodies simulate physics (gravity, forces, momentum) and physically respond to collisions. Use kinematic for players, platforms, and doors.

questions.q: What does the coefficient of restitution determine in a collision?
options: How far apart objects bounce after collision
How much kinetic energy is retained after a collision (bounciness)
The duration of the collision
The direction the objects separate
questions.explanation: Restitution (0 to 1) scales the relative velocity after collision: 0 is a dead stick, 1 is a rubber ball. It determines bounciness and energy retention without affecting the separation direction (that's the collision normal).

questions.q: Why might a physics engine use constraint solving instead of direct force application?
options: Forces are too slow to calculate
Constraints (e.g., joints, penetration) can be solved to maintain stability and prevent objects from separating or overlapping
Forces are only used in arcade games, not realistic ones
Constraints allow objects to move through each other safely
questions.explanation: Constraints directly manipulate velocities or positions to maintain joints (chains, hinges) and prevent penetration, offering stability that force-based approaches struggle with at low timesteps. Most modern engines combine both.

questions.q: In physics simulation, what problem does sub-stepping address?
options: Objects jittering due to floating-point precision
Instability and tunneling caused by large timesteps when forces are high
Objects moving in slow motion
Physics calculating faster than rendering
questions.explanation: Sub-stepping runs physics at a finer internal timestep (e.g., 4 substeps at 15ms) within each game frame, improving stability under high forces or fast movement without requiring a slower overall frame rate.

questions.q: What is the broad-phase step in collision detection and why is it necessary?
options: Detailed shape testing to calculate exact collision points
A quick culling step to eliminate obviously non-colliding pairs before expensive narrow-phase tests
The physics update step that precedes collision checks
The step that applies forces after collisions are detected
questions.explanation: Broad-phase quickly rejects pairs that can't possibly collide (e.g., AABB vs AABB) so expensive narrow-phase tests (e.g., polygon vs polygon) only run on promising candidates. This optimization is essential for many-object scenes.
